what_it_does: A standalone acceptance test that exercises Suite.resolve_surfaced and the background dispatch path with fake runners, verifying that an approve request returns promptly while the heavy build launch runs in a daemon thread, that the build still completes and emits terminal events, that a client disconnect cannot orphan it, that exactly-once dispatch holds under a race, that background-thread exceptions fail loud, and that a concurrency cap defers extra dispatches.
observations: Monkey-patches runtime.implement.launch and drive_dispatchable with fake functions to avoid real claude -p subprocesses.
Uses threading Events and Barriers to prove resolve returns while the runner is still blocked mid-flight.
Injects a fake self-build committer and records calls to prove the git checkpoint ran in the background thread.
Sets a 90-second faulthandler hard-exit guard to prevent silent hangs from daemon threads.
Tests seven labeled scenarios (i-vii) covering prompt return, background completion, disconnect survival, exactly-once, fail-loud, concurrency cap, and honest non-AUTO acks.
Relies on internal Suite methods such as _drive_dispatchable_bg, _self_build_commit, _default_suite_runner, and wire_wait_for_dispatch.
Uses COMPANY_WIRE_PERMISSION environment variable to arm/disarm wire edit permission.
Assertions are driven by the event log (decision.dispatch, decision.implemented, decision.verify, decision.deferred) and inbox status.
standouts: Barrier-based proof that resolve_surfaced returns before the slow fake runner finishes.
Exactly-once race test that spawns two background dispatch passes over the same seq and expects one decision.dispatch claim.
Fail-loud test where the whole background dispatch path raises and a decision.verify event is still emitted.
Deterministic concurrency-cap test that drives one background pass over two approved intents with cap=1.
Honest-ack test ensuring a non-AUTO build-intent reports status=surfaced with an empty dispatched list.
conventions: Heavy scenario docstrings at the top of each test block explain the bug, fix, and proof.
Global PASS counter incremented by a shared check helper.
Monkey-patched functions are saved and restored in try/finally blocks.
Temporary FsStore directories isolate each scenario.
Environment-based permission gating via arm/disarm helpers.
concerns: Monkey-patches global implementation module functions; while restored in finally, the pattern is not isolated across concurrent test runs.
Deep reliance on private Suite internals (_drive_dispatchable_bg, _self_build_commit, _default_suite_runner, _committer_calls) makes the test brittle to refactors.
The exactly-once race test depends on a 10-second barrier timeout as the expected losing-thread behavior, which is timing-sensitive.
The concurrency-cap test arms/disarms permission around resolve calls to suppress auto-triggers, adding setup complexity.
faulthandler.dump_traceback_later hard-exits at 90s, which can hide diagnostic information if a genuine hang occurs.
notes: A fake-runner acceptance suite that documents and guards the wire-async contract by proving the approve request decouples from the governed background build dispatch.
questions: What is the exact contract of Suite.wire_wait_for_dispatch and how does it know when a background pass is done?
How does the per-seq lock and durable claim inside dispatch_decision actually serialize concurrent passes?
What distinguishes consequence_class=decision_build from source_data in the gating logic, and where is that enforced?
How is the SSE stream wired to the event log to deliver terminal events independently of the HTTP request?
What is the production trigger that spawns _drive_dispatchable_bg threads, and how does it interact with the concurrency cap?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the actual content: it reproduces the synchronous-blocking bug and proves the async-decoupling fix while preserving governance guarantees.
apparent_themes: Decoupling HTTP request handling from long-running build execution
Exactly-once dispatch via durable claims and locks
Fail-loud error handling instead of silent thread death
Deterministic acceptance testing through dependency injection of fake runners and committers
Event-log-driven verification of asynchronous lifecycle completion
Honest status reporting without pretending success for non-dispatchable intents
intention_signals: The file is trying to demonstrate that moving the heavy build launch into a background thread does not weaken existing guarantees, and that the approve endpoint can return a prompt running-ack without orphaning builds on client disconnect.
novelty: It is a bug-reproduction-turned-acceptance-test that uses barrier-synchronized fake runners and event-log assertions to prove async dispatch behavior, including exactly-once and concurrency-cap properties.
contribution: A runnable, scenario-based acceptance test that captures and protects the wire-async contract: prompt ack, background completion, no orphaning, exactly-once dispatch, fail-loud exceptions, concurrency cap, and honest non-AUTO acks.
summary_for_embedding: Acceptance test for wire-async build dispatch decoupling that uses fake runners and event-log assertions to verify prompt return, background completion, exactly-once dispatch, fail-loud exceptions, concurrency cap, and honest acks.
intention_for_embedding: To prove that resolving an armed build-intent returns immediately while the governed dispatch/launch/verify/commit/surface path runs safely in a background thread.

symbols.does: Assertion helper that increments a global pass counter and prints an ok message.
symbols.description: check(label, cond) asserts the given condition, increments the module-level PASS counter on success, and prints a formatted ok line; failures raise with the label.

symbols.does: Factory that creates an isolated Suite with a temp store, discovered nodes, and injected fakes.
symbols.description: fresh_suite() builds a new FsStore in a temporary directory, discovers nodes, constructs a Suite, and replaces the self-build committer and default suite runner with test doubles so no real git commit or subprocess runs.

symbols.does: Records self-build commit calls and returns a dummy SHA.
symbols.description: _fake_commit(paths, msg) appends the paths and message to s._committer_calls and returns a fixed 40-character zero SHA, letting tests prove the background thread invoked the self-build checkpoint.

symbols.does: Sets the COMPANY_WIRE_PERMISSION environment variable to acceptEdits.
symbols.description: arm() enables wire edit permission by setting COMPANY_WIRE_PERMISSION=acceptEdits, used before scenarios that need an armed build-intent.
disarm() clears COMPANY_WIRE_PERMISSION from the environment, used to disable auto-triggers or wire edit permission between scenarios.

symbols.does: Returns all store events for a suite.
symbols.description: events(s) calls s.store.events_since(-1) to fetch the full event log so tests can inspect decision and resolve events.

symbols.does: Checks whether the event log contains an event of a given kind with matching fields.
symbols.description: has_event(s, kind, **match) scans events(s) for an entry whose kind equals the requested kind and whose fields match all provided key-value pairs.

symbols.does: Finds the seq of the resolve event associated with a surfaced item id.
symbols.description: resolve_seq(s, sid) searches the event log in reverse for a resolve event whose surfaced field equals sid and returns its seq, used to correlate dispatch events with the original approval.

symbols.does: Fake blocking runner that signals start and waits on a barrier before finishing.
symbols.description: slow_launch(decision, *, repo) sets runner_started, blocks on release_runner, then sets runner_finished and returns a successful implemented result, simulating a long claude -p subprocess so tests can prove resolve returned while the runner is mid-flight.

symbols.does: Fast fake runner that always returns a successful implemented result.
symbols.description: good_launch(decision, *, repo) immediately returns a finished, successful implemented result with changed_files and acceptEdits permission mode, used as a simple fake runner for background-completion and disconnect tests.

symbols.does: Fake runner that synchronizes both racing threads and counts how many times it runs.
symbols.description: counting_launch(decision, *, repo) waits on a two-thread barrier to maximize the race window, then increments a shared launch_count under a lock and returns success, enabling the exactly-once test to detect any double-launch.

symbols.does: Fake outer dispatch function that raises a RuntimeError.
symbols.description: exploding_drive(*a, **k) raises RuntimeError with a simulated catastrophic failure message, patched onto impl.drive_dispatchable so the fail-loud test can verify the background thread emits a decision.verify event instead of dying silently.
